title: Synthesis: Min, Max & the Whole Toolkit

beats.prompt: Cumulative recall across the whole arc. Match each headline result to its value.

feedback.correct: Every load-bearing headline from the arc: Var(die) = 35/12, Var(sum) = 35/6, ρ = 4/5, equicorrelation floor −1/2.
hints: Each card is one result from a different lesson of this concept.
Var(die) = 91/6 − 49/4; Var(sum) with Cov = 0; ρ = 12/15; the floor from 1 + 2ρ ≥ 0.

beats.prompt: The converse trap, one more time. Match each statement to its completion.

pairs.left: Cov(X, X²) with X symmetric about 0

feedback.correct: Cov(X,X²) = 0 yet X, X² are dependent; independence forces Cov = 0 — but the arrow runs one way only.
hints: The L4 converse trap: a Cov value, a dependence verdict, and a one-way direction.
Symmetry ⇒ Cov(X,X²) = 0, but Y = X² is computed from X; independent ⇒ Cov = 0, not the reverse.
0; dependent; independent ⇒ Cov = 0 (#7).

beats.prompt: Draw two numbers, keep the smaller (min) and the larger (max). How correlated are min and max?

options: ρ = 1 — min can never exceed max, locked together
ρ = 0 — which draw is bigger is random, so unrelated
ρ = 1/2 — positively but not perfectly correlated

ρ = 1 — min can never exceed max, locked.note: Let's test it — the ordering Y ≤ Z is real but doesn't make them move in lockstep. A big draw and a small draw raise the max while barely touching the min. ρ is only 1/2 (#8).

ρ = 0 — which draw is bigger is random,.note: Let's test it — they share the same two numbers, so they can't be independent. In fact YZ = X₁X₂ always, pinning Cov = 1/36 (#8).

ρ = 1/2 — positively but not perfectly c.note: Good instinct — they co-move, but the ordering doesn't lock them. Cov = 1/36, ρ = 1/2 (#8).

beats.prompt: Draw more (min, max) pairs and watch the cloud build. Every point lands on or above the diagonal (max ≥ min); the running ρ climbs and settles near 1/2 — a fat cloud, not a line.

hero.structuralReadout: Fat cloud above the diagonal — min and max co-move at ρ = 1/2, not 1.

feedback.correct: A fat cloud, not a line — min and max co-move at ρ = 1/2 despite the hard min ≤ max boundary. (The exact continuous result: Cov = 1/36, ρ = 1/2.)
hints: Tap 'draw' to add (min, max) points from iid-uniform pairs.
Every point sits on or above the y = x diagonal (max ≥ min); the cloud is fat, not a line.
Running ρ settles near 1/2 — far from the ρ = 1 reference line (#8).

beats.prompt: Two iid Uniform(0,1) draws; Y = min, Z = max. Using the identity YZ = min·max = X₁·X₂ (handed to you), what is E[YZ]?

feedback.correct: YZ = min·max = X₁·X₂ always; X₁, X₂ independent ⇒ E[YZ] = E[X₁]E[X₂] = (1/2)(1/2) = 1/4.
hints: min·max equals the product of the ORIGINAL two draws, every time.
YZ = X₁X₂; independent ⇒ E[YZ] = E[X₁]E[X₂] = (1/2)(1/2). Do NOT use E[Y]E[Z] — Y and Z are dependent.

beats.prompt: A spaced re-test before the mastery — read the card, then continue.

interaction.title: ρ denominator = √(product), not the product
interaction.body: Recall ρ = Cov / √(Var_Y · Var_Z). The denominator is the SQUARE ROOT of the product of the variances, not the product itself. When Var_Y = Var_Z (as for min and max, both 1/18), √(Var_Y·Var_Z) = √((1/18)²) = 1/18 — the √ collapses and ρ comes out rational. Dividing by the bare product 1/324 (or its root 1/648) is the classic slip.

feedback.correct: ρ denominator = √(Var_Y·Var_Z); when the variances are equal it collapses to that single variance.
hints: Read the card.
Denominator is √(product), not the product.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Mastery — two parts. Part A: the order-statistic covariance and correlation. Part B: pick the right tool for a re-surfaced bilinearity question.

interaction.scenario: Part A: two iid Uniform(0,1); Y = min, Z = max. With E[YZ] = 1/4, E[Y] = 1/3, E[Z] = 2/3, and Var(Y) = Var(Z) = 1/18, give Cov(Y,Z) and ρ(Y,Z). Part B: two independent fair dice; let S = X₁ + X₂. Give Cov(X₁, S).

feedback.correct: Part A: Cov = 1/4 − (1/3)(2/3) = 1/4 − 2/9 = 1/36; ρ = (1/36)/√((1/18)(1/18)) = (1/36)/(1/18) = 1/2 — rational because Var(Y) = Var(Z), so the √ collapses. Part B: Cov(X₁,S) = Cov(X₁,X₁) + Cov(X₁,X₂) = Var(X₁) + 0 = 35/12 — you picked the bilinearity tool; a variable always covaries perfectly with itself.
hints: Part A: Cov = E[YZ] − E[Y]E[Z] (subtract the dependent product); ρ denominator is a single √ of the product of variances. Part B: S contains X₁, so it's 'covariance with a sum', not an independence question.
Part A: Cov = 1/4 − (1/3)(2/3) = 1/36; with Var_Y = Var_Z = 1/18, √(Var·Var) = 1/18 (NOT the product 1/324, and not its root 1/648). Part B: Cov(X₁,X₁) + Cov(X₁,X₂) = Var(X₁) + 0.

beats.interviewNote: Interview framing: 'Correlation of min and max of two iid uniforms' is a Green-Book classic — the trick is YZ = min·max = X₁X₂, so E[YZ] = E[X₁]E[X₂] = 1/4 (NOT E[Y]E[Z] = 2/9, since Y, Z are dependent), giving Cov = 1/36 and ρ = 1/2. The whole toolkit — variance, the covariance leftover, the Var(X+Y) cross term, the unit-free ρ, and the PSD bounds — is one connected set of moves, picked apart on sight by which object the question asks for.
beats.prompt: One toolkit, told apart on sight.

feedback.correct: Variance, the covariance leftover, the cross term in Var(X+Y), the unit-free ρ, and the PSD bounds — one connected toolkit. The capstone move: read which object the question asks for, then reach for the matching tool.
hints: Read each recap card.
Every result this concept taught is one move in a single connected toolkit.
Tap to finish.
